text: The surgeon creates a large flap across the abdominal area during a bilateral inguinal hernia repair. Two twelve by sixteen pro group meshes are visible in the abdomen, along with sutures prepared for the procedure. The medial dissection is initiated as part of the repair process.

The surgeon dissects down the midline to locate the pubis and Cooper's ligament, then switches to lateral dissection while maintaining preperitoneal fat on the abdominal wall. The cord structures are visible, and the surgeon reduces a small direct hernia without an indirect sac, revealing the peritoneal flap. Parietalization is performed, and a hole created during the procedure is closed before completion.

The surgeon carefully separates the bladder from the fat overlying the iliac vessels, maintaining a good plane without skeletonizing the vessels. A search for a femoral hernia reveals mostly nodal tissue. The surgeon then parietalizes the cord on the right side and identifies a sizable lipoma, which is likely causing the patient's symptoms. The surgeon checks the cord to ensure the testicle is not inadvertently pulled out, achieving a successful separation.

The surgeon continues to mobilize the lipoma on the right side, ensuring it can sit beneath the mesh without lifting the inferior edge. The lipoma is fully mobilized without compromising blood supply, allowing room for mesh placement. The procedure moves to the left side, where the surgeon sweeps the cord structures down, revealing no indirect sac, and works towards a small left-sided direct lipoma or hernia.

The surgeon identifies the plane between the bladder and fat over the iliac vessels, prioritizing space for the mesh's inferior edge. The space is developed to allow the mesh to fit snugly along the iliacs while preserving the fat. The surgeon then locates the left-sided lipoma, reducing it by sweeping the cord structures off without pulling the testicle. A blood vessel within the canal is ligated to facilitate further reduction of the lipoma for mesh placement.

The surgeon fully reduces the lipoma, ensuring ample space for mesh placement. The final view shows the placement of a twelve by sixteen mesh on the right side, followed by the placement of the left-sided mesh.

The surgeon ensures the interior edges of the mesh oppose the sidewall to prevent tissue from sliding underneath. The peritoneal flap is closed, starting from the middle, then moving to the left and right. A small rent near the VAS joining the cord structures is repaired with a 3-0 Vicryl suture. The lipoma is placed back into the flap to sit on the inner surface of the mesh.

I did not have a Foley in for this case. I used to always for my inguinal hernias, and then I kept doing them for my bilaterals. I have since stopped completely. I'm happy with that decision. The patient really had no issues after this operation, was able to be discharged pretty shortly thereafter with no urinary symptoms, it used to always be a concern of mine. There I put the right lipoma back in sight, the flap, and go ahead and close this in the same fashion.
The surgeon uses an angiocath to apply suction to the central flap, compressing it with a grasper to remove gas and ensure the mesh remains flat without folding.
